title: Pantry Raid Recipes That Go Beyond Basic Survival
full_text: Pantry Raid Recipes That Go Beyond Basic Survival - Palate Asia Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: Pantry Raid Recipes That Go Beyond Basic Survival 1 01 Pantry Raid Recipes That Go Beyond Basic Survival Main // Eat // Drink // Reviews // Insider // At Home // About Us // Download Our Magazine // Q2 2026 // Q1 2026 // Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 // Home Eat Pantry Raid Recipes That Go Beyond Basic Survival Pantry Raid Recipes That Go Beyond Basic Survival Colin Gomez Eat ,  At Home 3 weeks ago Share Cooking from the pantry isn’t about making do. It’s about knowing how to build something coherent from what’s already there. There comes a point in every household where the fridge might start to look a little bit… existential, shall we say. You’ll have half a lemon sitting in the drawer. Some wilted greens that look closer to the compost than they do a salad. A lonesome egg rolling around there in the back somewhere. But the pantry? No. The pantry is a lot more resolute than its frigid kitchen companion. The home of rice, beans, canned tomatoes , tuna, and instant noodles, the pantry is chock-full of shelf-stable staples ready to be called into action. When you picture a pantry-based meal, you might conjure up images of desperate survival dinners concocted purely for sustenance, ones that can’t afford to take into consideration matters as frivolous as taste or enjoyment. But that is, as you’ll soon find out, merely a circumstance of ignorance rather than any sort of veracity. The fact of the matter is that, with a little bit of elbow grease and know-how, a pantry raid meal can make for some genuinely satisfying dinners that are more than the sum of their parts. Here are three recipes that you can adjust to your tastes to give a try the next time the refrigerator is looking a little threadbare. Photography: Towfiqu barbhuiya | Pexels You can do a lot with a can of beans Smoky Tomato Butter Beans on Toast There’s a rustic sort of comfort that comes to mind when I picture beans being simmered in tomatoes. It’s got this vaguely Mediterranean feel about it, and is characteristic of the way simple peasant food tends to circle back around to being chic again. That being said, this is a meal that’ll come across as deceptively expensive considering the bulk of it came out of a can. Ingredients (Serves 2) 1 can butter beans or cannellini beans 1 can chopped tomatoes 2 garlic cloves, minced 1 tsp smoked paprika Chilli flakes Olive oil Salt and black pepper Bread, toasted Optional: Anchovies Parmesan Capers Method Heat olive oil in a pan and cook garlic until fragrant. Add smoked paprika and chilli flakes and let them toast briefly. Pour in tomatoes and simmer for 10–15 minutes. Add beans and lightly mash some of them for texture. Season and spoon over toast. The thing about this dish isn’t so much its complexity as it is its depth. By blooming the spices in oil, the whole thing grows a smoky backbone that’ll underpin all the other flavours, while the beans soften into a delightful compromise between stew and spread. Splash on a healthy helping of olive oil and all of a sudden you’ve got the kind of meal your girlfriends drag you out for on a late Sunday morning that you’re liable to wash down with more than a couple of mimosas. Photography: Towfiqu barbhuiya | Pexels Who says canned tuna only belongs in sandwiches? Crispy Rice with Tuna, Corn, and Chilli Crisp One often overlooked component of cooking is texture, and no, that’s not just my autism speaking, thank you very much. Flavour obviously matters, but the texture of something can often be the defining factor that separates it from other dishes that do much the same thing. Crunch, in particular, is an unmistakable way to make a meal feel considered, as this next dish will attest to. Ingredients (Serves 2) 2 cups cooked rice 1 can tuna 1/2 cup canned corn 1 tbsp soy sauce 1 tbsp mayonnaise Chilli crisp or chilli oil Lime juice or vinegar Optional: Fried egg Seaweed Fried shallots Method Press rice into a hot, oiled pan and cook until crisp underneath. Mix tuna with mayonnaise and soy sauce. Spoon tuna and corn over the crispy rice. Finish with chilli crisp and lime juice or vinegar. This whole thing works because everything in it brings something a little different to the table. You’ve got the rice crackling in your mouth, while the tuna brings with it all that good savoury flavour. The corn adds just a little sweetness to soften things up, and the acidity makes a last-minute appearance to save things from feeling too heavy. This is also a meal that lends itself well to improvisation, so if you have something that’s been sitting in your fridge for a while and you haven’t a clue how to use it, feel free to toss it in. Photography: Polina
